Fern Por-rrrcAr- PnACrrcEs C oMMrssroN
-128 J Street o Suite (r20 o Sacramento. CA g-5Ull-2329
(916) -122--5660 o Fax (9t6) 312-0886
September 2,2008
William G. Brennan
Executive Director
New Motor Vehicle Board
1507 2l't Srreet; Suite 300
Sacramento, California 9581 I
Re:
Your Request for Advice
Our File No. ,{-08-144
Dear Mr. Brennan:
This letter responds to your request for advice regarding the gift provisions of the
Political Reform Act (the "Act").I
QUESTTON
Do the provisions of Regulation 18944.2, as recently revised, apply to the New Motor
Vehicle Board's use of a conference room provided by a party to a pendirg action for
mandatory settlement conferences or other proceedings?
CONCLUSION
No. A conference room provided by an outside party for use by the New Motor
Vehicle Board in holding its proceedings does not provide a personal benefit to an official
of the agency and is therefore not a gift under the Act.
FACTST
In 2006. the New Motor Vehicle Board lthe "Board") requested advice o1 whether it
could accept offers from litigants to Llse their conference rooms fbr mandatory settlement
conferences that were cllrrently being held only in state offices. Pursuant to your request. we
advised that "[ilf the requirements of regulationl894l.2 are met, the board may accept these
I The Political Refirrm Act is contained in Government Code Secrions til(X)0 through 91014. All
stotutory ret'erences are kl the G<lvernment Code. unless otherwise indicatecl. The regulatioris of the Fair
Politieal Practices Commission are contained in Sections llill0 through ltt997 0f Title 2 ()f the Califirrnia Code
of Regulations. All regtrlakrry ret'erences are to Title 2. Division 6 of the Calitirrnia C6de of Regulati6,s.
runless otherwise indicated.
r Actclitionally tacts were provided in our telephr)ne conversation of August 22. l(X)tt. in which one ()f
your atk)l'neys. Rrlly Riggenbach. provided further background intormation.


File No. A-08-144
Page No. 2
offers and no individual board member will be considered to have received a gift. The gift
will be considered made to the agency and not its members." (Weinberg Advice Letter, I-06-
032)
Since that time, the Board has been using litigants' offices space and/or meeting
rooms for purposes of presiding over mandatory settlement conferences, merit hearingf and
law and motion hearings while complying with the requirements of former Regulation
18944.2 as advised by Commission staff. You would now like to know it in t-igfrt of the
recent changes to Regulation L8944.2. the revised section applies to the Board's use of
conference rooms provided by parties to a pending action. You have indicated that the parties
must first stipulate to the use of a conference room provided by one of the parties before the
Board will agree to its use.
AI{ALYSIS
Section 82028 defines gift as ". . . any payment that confers a personal benefit on the
recipient, to the extent that consideration of equal or greater value is not received . . ..,,
Regulation 18944.2, as- amended, applies to situations where an agency receives something
of value from an outside source, and it sets forth the condition, *h"r" it *itt not ..be
considered a reportable or limited gift [under the Act] to an individual public official,.
although the fficial receives a personal benefitfrom the paymenr." (dmphasis added.) In
short, Regulation 18944.2 applies only to payments thar meet the Act's difinition of gift
(those that provide a personal benefit to a public official).
Under the facts you have presented, there is nothing to indicate that a,,personal
benefit" will be received by any Board official in his or hei use of the conference room. The
benefit, it appears, is being provided to the agency in that by receiving use of the room the
agency will not have to search to obtain sirnilar space in another buildlng and may even
avoid the cost of renting space at some other faciiity. The officials wholarticipate in each
proceeding' we assume, will always be provided with a room in which to perform their
duties. The fact that their participation will take place in a room providedby an outside
source rather than in a room acquired from state resources or with state funds does not
provide a personal benefit to them. our advice is limited to the actual use of the conference
room itself and does not include any items that may be tangential to its use, such as food or
beverages, which clearly do provide a personal benefit and would be considered gifts.
If you have other qllestions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660.
Sincerely,
Scott Hallabrin
C
By: Will
rt
wJLjgl
Senior
, Legal Division
